findings.summary: 1131 LOC component with 45+ custom hook imports and 30+ destructured props. Despite extraction of hooks and sections, the component is still a massive wiring layer that passes state between dozens of hooks. The contextValue construction alone spans ~150 lines. This is the canonical example of a component that has outgrown its abstraction boundary.

ShotImageManagerProps is composed from 6 sub-interfaces (good pattern) but the total field count is still enormous. ShotBatchItemMobileProps has 16 fields. The sub-grouping documents the complexity but doesn't reduce it - consumers still need to pass all these props through.

VideoPortionEditorProps has 34 fields including motion settings, LoRA management, generation actions, and segment editing. The SegmentThumbnail inner component (90+ lines with its own effects) should be extracted. Multiple useEffect hooks and complex inline video element manipulation compound the issue.

SegmentSettingsFormProps has 39 fields. Many are optional but mutually interacting (e.g., hasOverride map with 12 boolean fields, shotDefaults object, structure video context with nested objects). The interface tries to serve too many use cases - timeline mode, batch mode, regeneration mode - through a single prop surface.

MediaGalleryProps has 33 fields plus GalleryConfig with 15 boolean flags. The GalleryConfig extraction is a good move (grouping booleans), but 15 boolean config flags suggest the component serves too many display variants. GeneratedImageWithMetadata has 25+ fields with optional types everywhere.

useLoraManager (455 LOC) and useLoraSync (261 LOC) implement nearly identical LoRA management logic (add, remove, strength change, save/load to project, trigger words, header actions). useLoraSync exists as a thin adapter between context-managed state and the LoRA UI, but duplicates most of useLoraManager's logic. This is the 'two similar functions' pattern that could be one.

The hook manages a 5-state machine (idle/loading/ready/saving/error) with two parallel code paths (React Query mode and custom load/save mode) controlled by isCustomMode flag. The interaction between pending edits, loading, entity changes, and debounced saves creates many edge cases. The getLatestSettings trick using setSettings as a reader is clever but non-obvious.

This hook composes 16 sub-hooks into a single return object. While each sub-hook is well-named, the composed props interface (UseSharedLightboxStateProps) spans 5 sub-interfaces with 40+ total fields. The hook is essentially a wiring layer that doesn't add logic - it just calls hooks and returns their values in a grouped structure.

checkPageVisibility() is an empty function body. The file has 0 importers yet is 125 LOC of debug utilities with verbose console.error logging patterns. Each method logs the same error object in 5 different formats (error, errorMessage, errorCode, errorDetails, errorHint). Classic AI-generated 'cover all bases' approach.

139 LOC debug utility with 0 importers. Uses require() inside a function (non-standard in ESM context), emojis in console.log messages, and hardcoded localStorage key checks ('projects', 'selectedProject', 'auth', 'user') that may not match actual keys. The forceProjectRecovery function uses setTimeout chains with console.log countdown - a pattern that looks auto-generated.

131 LOC with heavy (window as any) casting. The showCacheHelp() function is entirely static console.log messages with emoji numbering (1-4) explaining what to look for. The startCacheWatch interval captures DOM state every second but the log line for state changes was removed (empty if block). Over-mocked tests (9 mocks vs 8 assertions) per existing findings.

119 LOC with 0 importers (dynamically imported from main.tsx). The logVideoStateChanges function builds a changes array but never outputs it (the console.log was removed, leaving the function doing work for nothing). The setInterval polls DOM every second in development - wasteful for a monitor that produces no output.

4 boolean useState hooks (isBrandFlash, isCreateProjectModalOpen, isProjectSettingsModalOpen, isReferralModalOpen) plus additional state for viewport width and sticky header. The brand flash logic uses a timeout ref that's manually cleaned up. These could be consolidated into a single modal state discriminated union.

The deduplication useEffect on line 90 watches selectedLoras and conditionally calls setSelectedLoras - which changes selectedLoras - creating a potential re-trigger. The guard (uniqueMap.size !== selectedLoras.length) prevents infinite loops but the pattern is fragile. Additionally, shouldApplyDefaults on line 351 checks 'persistenceSettings.loras.length >= 0' which is always true for any array.

Lines 78-81 defensively check typeof for callback props (onModeChange, onPresetRemove, onPresetSelect, onPhaseConfigChange) and replace with no-ops. The comment says 'some callsites may pass non-functions via any'. This is working around TypeScript unsafety rather than fixing the callsites - it hides bugs at the boundary.

Position operations (updatePosition, commitPositions, reorder) use handleError + throw. Pair data operations (updatePairPrompts, updatePairPromptsByIndex) use console.error + throw. Enhanced prompt operations mix both patterns within the same hook. The clearAllEnhancedPrompts function loops with individual awaits (no batching) and silently continues on per-item failures.

useAddImageToShot handles three distinct behaviors based on timelineFrame (undefined/null/number) - add with auto-position, add without position, and add with explicit position. useAddImageToShotWithoutPosition wraps useAddImageToShot. usePositionExistingGenerationInShot handles a fourth case. The naming doesn't clearly convey that 'add' means 'create junction record' while 'position' means 'set frame on existing record'.

Numerous section comments that restate the obvious: '// Use the LoRA sync hook - now using context values', '// Use generation actions hook', '// Alias for backwards compatibility with existing code', '// Auto-disable turbo mode when there are more than 2 images'. The emoji-prefixed comments (e.g., target emoji PERF FIX) appear 8+ times and read like AI-generated annotations.

Re-exports EditAdvancedSettings and QwenEditModel from editSettingsTypes with a comment 'Re-export for backwards compatibility'. The circular import avoidance comment for StrokeOverlayHandle is legitimate, but the file serves two roles: defining core brush/edit types AND re-exporting settings types. With 11 importers this is a high-traffic module.

438 LOC type definition file where most interfaces have multi-line JSDoc on every field. TravelBetweenImagesTaskParams has ~60 fields with deprecation notices, code examples in docstrings, and commented section headers. While documentation is good, many docstrings restate the field name: '/** Default/base prompt used for all segments */ base_prompt?: string'. The @deprecated annotations are useful but the volume suggests AI-generated expansion.

The useEffect on line 62 revokes old URLs and creates new ones, but the cleanup function references the stale internalPreviewUrls from the closure at cleanup time. Since the effect runs when internalFiles changes, and the first lines revoke all URLs and clear the array, there's a potential double-revoke on the same URLs.

Uses 'actions' as a catch-all for the hook return (useVariantActions). Variables like 'mobileInfoVariant', 'actions.handleLoadSettings', 'actions.handleLoadImages' are adequate but 'actions' itself is generic. The 'prevRelFilterRef' trick to reset pagination on filter change (lines 84-88) uses a ref-based pattern instead of useEffect - correct but unusual.

Well-structured routing component that delegates to specialized detail components based on task type. The if-chain for task type routing is clear and each branch is self-documenting. The parsedParams/metadata construction at the bottom for the fallback case could be extracted but is reasonably clear.

Clean separation between compute helpers (computeIsMobile, computeIsTablet, computeIsTouchDevice) and hooks (useIsMobile, useIsTablet, useDeviceInfo). The _useIsPhone private function is unused externally but referenced by useDeviceInfo's isPhone field. isMobileUA utility export is well-documented.

Excellent function naming: updatePosition, commitPositions, reorder, deleteItem, unpositionItem, addItem, clearAllEnhancedPrompts. The 'positioned' vs 'unpositioned' distinction is domain-specific and consistently used. Type aliases (PairPrompts, SegmentOverrides) are clear.

Public API is well-named: updateField, updateFields, save, saveImmediate, revert, reset, initializeFrom. Status type is a clear discriminated union. Internal naming like 'debouncedSave.hasPendingFor' communicates intent well. The 'getLatestSettings' function name obscures its setSettings-based implementation but the concept is clear.

Straightforward page component with clear state management. The setTimeout for scrollIntoView (line 49) is a minor code smell but has a comment explaining it. Clean conditional rendering with selectedBatchId gating. Error handling delegated properly to handleError.

Clean entry point with clear initialization order. The import.meta.env.NODE_ENV vs import.meta.env.DEV inconsistency is minor (line 17 vs 22). Dark mode initialization from localStorage before React render prevents flash correctly.

Clean barrel export with well-organized sections (types, service, tracker, preloader). The PRIORITY_VALUES legacy alias comment is helpful. 9 importers suggest this is a well-used module. Clear separation of concerns.

Well-organized constants with clear names. STATUS_GROUPS mapping is self-documenting. TASK_NAME_ABBREVIATIONS is a clean lookup table. The 'as const' assertions are appropriate. KNOWN_IMAGE_TASK_TYPES comment explains the purpose (fallback when DB doesn't have content_type).
Minimal, focused constants file with descriptive names and comments. GRID_COLS_CLASSES mapping is clean. Timing and offset constants are well-named with units in comments.

ImageTransform interface with clear field names and comments explaining units (percentage, degrees). DEFAULT_TRANSFORM is well-structured. UseRepositionModeProps has 17 fields but they're well-named. The dragHandlers grouping in the return type is a good pattern.

Clean component with discriminated union state (DownloadState). The conditional fetch (open ? variantId : null) is a good pattern for avoiding unnecessary queries. Error handling in handleDownloadGif is consistent. The renderContent pattern keeps JSX organized.

Consistent optimistic update pattern with rollback on error. User-friendly error messages with network condition detection. The duplicate key check with specific constraint name is good. However, the onError in useRemoveImageFromShot exposes raw error.message to user while useAddImageToShot has more sophisticated message mapping.

Complex initialization logic for tablet vs phone vs desktop pane behavior. The hydration effect handles multiple device types with different rules (tablets get one lock max, phones get none). The multiple useState calls for dimensions that aren't persisted could use useReducer. State is well-scoped but the interaction between isSmallMobile, isTablet, and paneLocks creates implicit behavior.

MotionControl (seen in query.json with structural finding) implements preset chips, browse modal, and Basic/Advanced tabs - functionality that's also in the shared MotionPresetSelector component. The file defines its own BUILTIN_I2V_PRESET and BUILTIN_VACE_PRESET alongside MotionPresetSelector's similar pattern. This is a clear duplication that the shared component was meant to replace.

stripModeFromPhaseConfig and extractPairOverrides are well-named. buildBasicModePhaseConfig name is clear but the hardcoded model string inside it ('wan_2_2_i2v_lightning_baseline_2_2_2') is a magic constant. PREPROCESSING_MAP uses short keys without documentation. The overall file mixes type re-exports, utility functions, and service construction.

HoverScrubVideoProps has 20 fields including 7 boolean behavior flags (disableScrubbing, loadOnDemand, thumbnailMode, autoplayOnHover, posterOnlyUntilClick, playOnClick, showSpeedControls). These flags interact and some are mutually exclusive (thumbnailMode disables scrubbing). The component tries to serve too many display modes through boolean configuration.

Lines 58-61 perform a render-phase state update (checking prev refs and calling setIsVideoReady). While this is a known React pattern to avoid useEffect, it can be surprising. The component has clean callback definitions and the playbackStart/playbackEnd boundary enforcement in useEffect is well-structured.

Each step component (IntroductionStep, CommunityStep, etc.) follows an identical template: DialogHeader with icon + title, content div, button. The getStepColors function cycles through 3 color combos. This is a typical AI-generated component where a template was applied to each step rather than creating a reusable StepLayout component.

Good use of domain terms (preloadImage, editToolMediaPersistence, variantToGenerationRow). The preloadedImageRef pattern is a bit unusual for a module-level mutable ref. The useProjectGenerations call with inline options object is readable. TOOL_TYPE constant at module level is clean.

ImageLightboxProps has 40+ fields (visible in first 100 lines, continues beyond). This is the consumer-facing API that must be assembled by every callsite. While the internal hook composition (useSharedLightboxState, useImageEditOrchestrator) is well-factored, the external props surface is still massive.

The component destructures 30+ props directly in the function signature. Backward-compatibility aliases at the module level (DEFAULT_JOIN_CLIPS_PHASE_CONFIG, BUILTIN_JOIN_CLIPS_DEFAULT_ID) add cognitive overhead. The component mixes settings form rendering with complex constraint calculations for frame limits.
